text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel
Riverside Family Practice

12 Thames View, London, SE1 3AB
Dear Dr Patel
Emily Carter, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: H729314 NHS No. 9384726150
Flat 3, 27 Willow Court, London, SE1 5LD 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. Since my last review in March this year, there has been a noticeable deterioration in day-to-day functioning, chiefly related to recent events associated with falls or minor injuries. The family reports several occasions over the past six weeks where brief lapses and morning jerks have led to unsteadiness, with two incidents resulting in forehead bruising and one involving a cut to the right elbow that required steri-strips at the local urgent care centre. No prolonged loss of consciousness was reported during these episodes, but there has been increasing concern about safety in the bathroom and on stairs. 

Her seizure control is variable but the family feels it is acceptable, she continues to experience petit mal on a daily basis, myoclonic jerks in morning clusters, and occasional generalised convulsions seizures, this pattern has remained unchanged for several years. However, despite the long-standing pattern, the recent increase in falls and minor injuries suggests a functional decline and reduced resilience around the events. The family notes greater post-event fatigue, slower recovery over the remainder of the day, and a new hesitancy to leave the house unaccompanied. Sleep has been fragmented with earlier morning waking, and she reports increased stress at work which may be contributory. There is no history of medication non-adherence, intercurrent infection, or alcohol excess. 

Current treatment as provided by the family and checked against records: Levetiracetam 1500 mg b.d., Valproate 500 mg b.d., Clobazam 10 mg nocte, and Lamotrigine 100 mg b.d. She denies new adverse effects beyond intermittent dizziness and mild daytime somnolence. No rash, no new gastrointestinal symptoms, and weight is stable. 

Examination today revealed no focal neurological deficits. Gait was cautious with mild postural instability on tandem stance. Cardiovascular and systemic examination were unremarkable. 

In view of the recent deterioration characterised by falls and minor injuries, I have advised immediate practical safety measures at home (supervision on stairs and in the bathroom, use of a shower chair, and avoidance of carrying hot liquids during early morning periods). I have asked the family to maintain a detailed seizure and fall diary, noting time of day, potential triggers, recovery time, and whether the events occur on rising. I will arrange an expedited ambulatory EEG to evaluate for changes in interictal activity and to correlate with reported events, and I have requested an up-to-date full blood count, U&E, LFTs, and antiepileptic drug levels where relevant. We discussed referral to physiotherapy for balance assessment and to occupational therapy for home hazard review; the patient is agreeable. 

We also discussed mental wellbeing; she reports increased anxiety about further falls. I have signposted her to local epilepsy support services for counselling and practical advice. I will review her again in 8–10 weeks, sooner if there are further injuries, any increase in convulsive events, or episodes lasting longer than usual. The family has been given emergency advice and contact details for our service. 

Thank you for your ongoing care and for forwarding any interim observations, especially regarding further falls or changes in daily functioning.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: she continues to experience petit mal on a daily basis, myoclonic jerks in morning clusters, and occasional generalised convulsions seizures

Her seizure control is variable but the family feels it is acceptable, she continues to experience petit mal on a daily basis, myoclonic jerks in morning clusters, and occasional generalised convulsions seizures, this pattern has remained unchanged for several years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly describes current seizure patterns: "she continues to experience petit mal on a daily basis, myoclonic jerks in morning clusters, and occasional generalised convulsions." We must select the highest frequency per the rule. The daily petit mal indicates at least 1 seizure per day. The myoclonic events are in clusters but without a numeric frequency per day/week; thus cannot be quantified precisely for a higher rate. "Occasional" generalized convulsions are less frequent than daily. Therefore, the highest determinable frequency is daily. No numeric count beyond at least daily is provided, so label as 1 per day. Quote supports the daily frequency.
seizure_frequency_number: she continues to experience petit mal on a daily basis, myoclonic jerks in morning clusters, and occasional generalised convulsions seizures
